title: NOC 72204 – Telecommunications line and cable installers and repairers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 72204 professionals are the architects of Canada’s digital connectivity. As a TEER 2 occupation, these technicians install, repair, and maintain the complex aerial and underground networks that support high-speed internet, fiber optics, and cable television. In 2026, as Canada nears completion of its "Universal High-Speed Internet" mandate to connect 100% of rural and remote communities, these installers are classified as Essential Infrastructure Talent. For immigration, this role is a consistent target for Ontario’s OINP Employer Job Offer draws and the Federal Skilled Trades Program (FSTP).

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level2Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group722 – Electrical trades and electrical power line and cable workersMinor Group7220 – Electricians (except industrial and power system)NOC Code72204

duties: Installation: Install, remove, and maintain aerial and underground telephone, coaxial, and fiber optic cables for telecommunication distribution.
Splicing: Use specialized equipment to splice and repair various types and sizes of cables, ensuring minimal signal loss in fiber optic networks.
Equipment Maintenance: Repair or replace faulty cables, power supplies, amplifiers, and associated transmission hardware.
Inspection & Testing: Use cable testers and signal meters to inspect, monitor, and adjust distribution systems; analyze and record test results for quality assurance.
Structural Work: Assist in the erection and removal of telecommunication poles, towers, and support structures.
Field Operations: Climb ladders or support structures and work aloft; alternatively, work in confined spaces like trenches, tunnels, and crawl spaces.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is mandatory.

Education & Training:Completion of a four-year apprenticeship program OR a two-year college program in electronics or telecommunications.Alternatively, a combination of over three years of work experience and industry-related specialized courses may be accepted.
Completion of a four-year apprenticeship program OR a two-year college program in electronics or telecommunications.
Alternatively, a combination of over three years of work experience and industry-related specialized courses may be accepted.
Certifications:Most employers require Fall Protection and Confined Space Entry certifications.A valid driver’s license (often Class 5 or higher for larger bucket trucks) is essential.
Most employers require Fall Protection and Confined Space Entry certifications.
A valid driver’s license (often Class 5 or higher for larger bucket trucks) is essential.
Physical stamina for climbing and working in all weather conditions, along with good color vision (for wire identification), is required.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, senior Fiber Splicers and project leads in major hubs like Vancouver or Toronto often earn annual salaries between $80,000 and $95,000 inclusive of overtime and emergency call-out premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady demand for new technicians as the industry shifts from traditional copper networks to a fully fiber-based national grid. While automation is increasing in other sectors, the physical installation and repair of physical line infrastructure remain human-dependent, ensuring long-term job security for the next decade.
labour_market_demand: The demand for telecommunications line workers in 2026 is Steady to Good, particularly in provinces undergoing massive rural fiber expansions. In British Columbia (Lower Mainland) and Ontario, while the general urban market is balanced, there is a noted shortage of technicians specialized in Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH) splicing and high-capacity underground cabling. According to 2026 Job Bank data, over 94% of workers in this field are employed full-time, reflecting the industry's shift toward permanent infrastructure maintenance rather than just temporary installations.
The Fiber Revolution: Lead the deployment of cutting-edge XGS-PON and 5G backhaul infrastructure across diverse Canadian landscapes.
Exceptional Benefits: Over 95.8% of workers in this NOC receive comprehensive non-wage benefits, including employer-sponsored dental, medical, and pension plans.
Outdoor & Technical Mix: Perfect for professionals who enjoy a blend of physical field work—climbing poles or working in trenches—and high-precision technical splicing.
Strategic PR Pathways: This role is frequently included in targeted provincial draws, such as the OINP's "Skilled Trades" rounds, which prioritized this NOC as recently as February 2026.
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer (Foreign Worker Stream): Highly targeted. This NOC was specifically invited in the March 25, 2026 regional draws for Northern and Eastern Ontario.
Express Entry (Federal Skilled Trades Program): A reliable route for technicians with at least 2 years of work experience and a valid job offer or certificate of qualification.
BC PNP Skilled Worker: Priority for installers supporting the province’s connectivity projects in rural and coastal regions.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure for 33,000 skilled workers already in Canada, specifically targeting "Trade and Infrastructure" roles.
